section.title: Cleansing of Skin Diseases and Mildew
section.sectionRationale: Chapter thirteen diagnosed disease; this chapter provides the ritual for restoring a healed person to the camp and to worship, along with the parallel law for a house contaminated with mildew, closing the purity regulations concerning skin disease.
section.thesis: God provides an elaborate, joyful ritual for restoring a healed person to full community life, two birds, cedar, scarlet yarn, and hyssop, followed by sacrifices with blood and oil applied to ear, thumb, and toe just as in priestly ordination, teaching that restoration to fellowship with God and His people is as carefully provided for as exclusion was required.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the cleansing ritual with two birds outside the camp; the eighth day sacrifices restoring full access to the sanctuary; the provision for the poor; and the parallel law for a house contaminated with mildew.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“This is the law for the one afflicted with a skin disease on the day of his cleansing, when he is brought to the priest.
The priest is to go outside the camp to examine him, and if the skin disease of the afflicted person has healed,
the priest shall order that two live clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and {{ezov|hyssop}} be brought for the one to be cleansed.
Then the priest shall command that one of the birds be slaughtered over fresh water in a clay pot.
And he is to take the live bird together with the cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop, and dip them into the blood of the bird that was slaughtered over the fresh water.
Seven times he shall sprinkle the one to be cleansed of the skin disease. Then he shall pronounce him clean and release the live bird into the open field.

The one being cleansed must wash his clothes, shave off all his hair, and bathe with water; then he will be ceremonially clean. Afterward, he may enter the camp, but he must remain outside his tent for seven days.
On the seventh day he must shave off all his hair, his head, his beard, his eyebrows, and the rest of his hair. He must wash his clothes and bathe himself with water, and he will be clean.

units.label: The eighth day
units.note: blood and oil on ear, thumb, and toe

verses.text: On the eighth day he is to bring two unblemished male lambs, an unblemished ewe lamb a year old, a grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with olive oil, and one log of olive oil.
The priest who performs the cleansing shall present the one to be cleansed, together with these offerings, before the LORD at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
Then the priest is to take one of the male lambs and present it as a guilt offering, along with the log of olive oil; and he must wave them as a wave offering before the LORD.
Then he is to slaughter the lamb in the sanctuary area where the sin offering and burnt offering are slaughtered. Like the sin offering, the guilt offering belongs to the priest; it is most holy.
The priest is to take some of the blood from the guilt offering and put it on the right earlobe of the one to be cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.
Then the priest shall take some of the log of olive oil, pour it into his left palm,
dip his right forefinger into the oil in his left palm, and sprinkle some of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD.

And the priest is to put some of the oil remaining in his palm on the right earlobe of the one to be cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot, on top of the blood of the guilt offering.
The rest of the oil in his palm, the priest is to put on the head of the one to be cleansed, to make {{kipper|atonement}} for him before the LORD.
Then the priest is to sacrifice the sin offering and make atonement for the one to be cleansed from his uncleanness. After that, the priest shall slaughter the burnt offering
and offer it on the altar, with the grain offering, to make atonement for him, and he will be clean.

If, however, the person is poor and cannot afford these offerings, he is to take one male lamb as a guilt offering to be waved to make atonement for him, along with a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with olive oil for a grain offering, a log of olive oil,
and two turtledoves or two young pigeons, whichever he can afford, one to be a sin offering and the other a burnt offering.
On the eighth day he is to bring them for his cleansing to the priest at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting before the LORD.
The priest shall take the lamb for the guilt offering, along with the log of olive oil, and wave them as a wave offering before the LORD.

And after he slaughters the lamb for the guilt offering, the priest is to take some of the blood of the guilt offering and put it on the right earlobe of the one to be cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.
Then the priest is to pour some of the oil into his left palm
and sprinkle with his right forefinger some of the oil in his left palm seven times before the LORD.

The priest shall also put some of the oil in his palm on the right earlobe of the one to be cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot, on the same places as the blood of the guilt offering.
The rest of the oil in his palm, the priest is to put on the head of the one to be cleansed, to make atonement for him before the LORD.
Then he must sacrifice the turtledoves or young pigeons, whichever he can afford,
one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering, together with the grain offering. In this way the priest will make atonement before the LORD for the one to be cleansed.
This is the law for someone who has a skin disease and cannot afford the cost of his cleansing.”
Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
“When you enter the land of Canaan, which I am giving you as your possession, and I put a contamination of mildew into a house in that land,
the owner of the house shall come and tell the priest, ‘Something like mildew has appeared in my house.’
The priest must order that the house be cleared before he enters it to examine the mildew, so that nothing in the house will become unclean. After this, the priest shall go in to inspect the house.
He is to examine the house, and if the mildew on the walls consists of green or red depressions that appear to be beneath the surface of the wall,
the priest shall go outside the doorway of the house and close it up for seven days.
On the seventh day the priest is to return and inspect the house. If the mildew has spread on the walls,
he must order that the contaminated stones be pulled out and thrown into an unclean place outside the city.
And he shall have the inside of the house scraped completely and the plaster that is scraped off dumped into an unclean place outside the city.
So different stones must be obtained to replace the contaminated ones, as well as additional mortar to replaster the house.
If the mildew reappears in the house after the stones have been torn out and the house has been scraped and replastered,
the priest must come and inspect it. If the mildew has spread in the house, it is a destructive mildew; the house is unclean.
It must be torn down with its stones, its timbers, and all its plaster, and taken outside the city to an unclean place.
Anyone who enters the house during any of the days that it is closed up will be unclean until evening.
And anyone who sleeps in the house or eats in it must wash his clothes.
If, however, the priest comes and inspects it, and the mildew has not spread after the house has been replastered, he shall pronounce the house clean, because the mildew is gone.
He is to take two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop to purify the house;
and he shall slaughter one of the birds over fresh water in a clay pot.
Then he shall take the cedar wood, the hyssop, the scarlet yarn, and the live bird, dip them in the blood of the slaughtered bird and the fresh water, and sprinkle the house seven times.
And he shall cleanse the house with the bird's blood, the fresh water, the live bird, the cedar wood, the hyssop, and the scarlet yarn.
Finally, he is to release the live bird into the open fields outside the city. In this way he will make atonement for the house, and it will be clean.
This is the law for any infectious skin disease, for a scaly outbreak,
for mildew in clothing or in a house,
and for a swelling, rash, or spot,
to determine when something is clean or unclean. This is the law regarding skin diseases and mildew.”

ezov.gloss: Hyssop, a small bushy plant used throughout Scripture for sprinkling blood or water in cleansing rituals, including the Passover, Exodus 12:22, and later invoked by David's plea, purge me with hyssop, Psalm 51:7, as a symbol of thorough cleansing from sin.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover. Even after the disease itself had visibly healed, atonement still had to be made before the person was declared fully clean, showing that restoration to community and to worship required more than physical healing alone.

basic.context: Where chapter thirteen diagnosed disease, this chapter provides the joyful ritual for restoring a healed person to full community life. Two birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop are used outside the camp, followed eight days later by sacrifices whose blood and oil are applied to the healed person's ear, thumb, and toe, echoing exactly the ordination of priests. The chapter closes with the same careful pattern applied to a house contaminated with mildew.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes Israel's actual old covenant ritual for restoring a healed person and a contaminated house to cleanness; it is not a pattern for Christian healing or house blessing rituals today. Its abiding truth is that God cares as much about restoration and homecoming as He does about the exclusion that disease required, and that being clean before God, then as now, is never merely a private matter but restores full participation in community.
basic.originalLanguages: The ritual uses ezov, hyssop, the same plant used at the first Passover to apply blood to Israel's doorposts, and later invoked by David's own plea for cleansing from sin, purge me with hyssop, Psalm 51:7. Even after physical healing was confirmed, kaphar, atonement, still had to be made, showing that the disease's ceremonial weight went beyond mere physical illness.
basic.typology: That the healed leper receives blood and oil on ear, thumb, and toe, the very ritual used to ordain priests in chapters eight and nine, suggests that being restored from this defiling condition returns a person to something like the priestly, worshiping community in full. The two birds, one slain and one released alive carrying the blood, anticipate the pattern later fulfilled in the two goats of the Day of Atonement, chapter sixteen, one goat slain and one sent away bearing sin.
basic.christ: The hyssop used here recalls both the first Passover, Exodus 12:22, and points to Christ, our Passover Lamb, whose blood truly cleanses, echoed when hyssop appears again at the crucifixion, John 19:29. As the healed person was restored not only physically but ceremonially, fully welcomed back into worship, so Christ's cleansing restores sinners not merely to healing but to full fellowship and priestly access to God, 1 Peter 2:9.
basic.god: God's law provided joyful, thorough restoration for the very people this system had required to be excluded, showing that exclusion was never His final intention but a temporary measure protecting the community's holiness until healing came. His care extends to providing an accessible path for the poor, exactly as with earlier offerings, proving restoration was never withheld for lack of means.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12:22 describes hyssop used to apply the Passover lamb's blood to the doorposts. Psalm 51:7 has David plead, purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean, applying this ritual language to the cleansing of sin itself. John 19:29 records hyssop lifted to Jesus on the cross, tying His final moments to this whole pattern of cleansing.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows a God who provides thorough, joyful restoration for those healed from a defiling disease, welcoming them back fully into community and worship, never leaving exclusion as His last word. It anticipates Christ, our true Passover Lamb, whose blood, applied like the hyssop of this ritual, cleanses sinners fully and restores them to complete fellowship with God.

context.literary: This chapter directly answers chapter thirteen, moving from diagnosis of disease to the ritual of restoration, and forms a matched pair with it, both chapters closing with nearly identical summary statements, this is the law, verses 54-57 and 13:59.
context.historical: Priests functioned here not primarily as physicians but as those who authoritatively declared a person's status and mediated the ritual restoring them fully to community life and worship, a role central to Israel's social and religious fabric.
context.canonical: This ritual's blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe, identical to priestly ordination in chapter eight, and its use of hyssop, connecting to Passover and to David's prayer for cleansing in Psalm 51, weave this chapter into some of Scripture's most significant threads about atonement and restoration.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide the ritual by which a person healed of skin disease, and a house cleared of mildew, could be formally declared clean and restored to full community and worship, ensuring even the poor had accessible provision for this restoration.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes an actual old covenant ceremonial procedure for Israel; it does not prescribe a ritual for Christian healing or purification today. Its abiding principle is that God delights in full restoration for those who have been excluded, and that this restoration was always made accessible regardless of wealth.

debates.question: Why does the healed leper receive the same ear, thumb, and toe ritual used at priestly ordination, chapters 8 and 14:14?

views.label: A restoration to full covenant participation
views.case: Many interpreters see this deliberate echo as marking the healed person's full return to normal covenant life and worship, using the same symbolic consecration of hearing, doing, and walking applied to priests, showing that ordinary Israelites, too, were consecrated to serve and worship God with their whole selves.

views.label: A demonstration of the completeness of the person's cleansing
views.case: Others emphasize that the shared ritual pattern primarily communicates thoroughness, that this cleansing, like ordination, left no part of the person untouched by the process, rather than implying any priestly status specifically.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that this ritual was not a minor formality but a significant, whole person restoration to full standing before God and the community.

study.originalLanguages: The ritual's use of ezov, hyssop, deliberately echoes its use at the first Passover, Exodus 12:22, and anticipates its later invocation in David's plea for cleansing from sin, Psalm 51:7. That kaphar, atonement, was required even after physical healing was confirmed shows that this disease's weight was never merely medical but touched the person's whole standing before God and the community.

typology.type: Blood and oil applied to the healed person's ear, thumb, and toe
typology.fulfillment: Full restoration to worship and priestly access to God, fulfilled for all believers in Christ, 1 Peter 2:9.

typology.type: Hyssop used to apply cleansing blood
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our Passover Lamb, whose blood cleanses fully, echoed at the cross itself, John 19:29.

study.christ: The hyssop that applies cleansing blood here recalls the first Passover, Exodus 12:22, and reappears at the crucifixion, John 19:29, tying Christ's death directly to this entire pattern of cleansing through blood. Where this ritual restored a healed leper to full community and worship, 1 Peter 2:9 declares that believers in Christ are made a royal priesthood, a holy nation, restored fully to God through the greater cleansing Christ provides.
study.god: God delights in full, joyful restoration, providing an elaborate and generous ritual to welcome back those who had been excluded because of disease, never leaving exclusion as His final word. His provision for the poor within this very ritual shows a God whose restoration is never rationed according to wealth but made accessible to all.

crossReferences.note: Hyssop applies the Passover lamb's blood to Israel's doorposts, the same plant used in this chapter's cleansing ritual.
David pleads, purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean, applying this ritual language to the deeper cleansing of sin.
Believers in Christ are made a royal priesthood, a holy nation, echoing the priestly ordination pattern applied here to a healed person.

study.oneStory: This chapter shows God providing joyful, thorough, and accessible restoration for those healed from a defiling disease, welcoming them fully back into worship and community life. It anticipates Christ, our true Passover Lamb, whose blood, applied as surely as the hyssop of this ancient ritual, cleanses sinners completely and restores them to full and permanent fellowship with God.

quiz.q: What materials are used in the initial cleansing ritual performed outside the camp for a healed person?

options.text: Two live clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop.
options.note: Verse 4 lists exactly these materials for the initial ritual.

options.text: Oil and frankincense only.
options.note: Oil is used later in the eighth day ritual, verses 15 to 18; the initial ritual specifically requires birds, cedar, scarlet yarn, and hyssop.

options.text: A bull and a ram.
options.note: Larger animals appear in the eighth day sacrifices, verse 10, but the initial outside the camp ritual uses birds, not a bull or ram.

options.text: No specific materials are listed.
options.note: Verse 4 gives an explicit list of materials.

quiz.q: Where is the blood of the guilt offering applied to the healed person on the eighth day?

options.text: The right earlobe, the thumb of the right hand, and the big toe of the right foot.
options.note: Verse 14 specifies this exact application, identical to the ordination ritual of chapter 8.

options.text: Only the forehead.
options.note: The ritual specified here applies blood to ear, thumb, and toe, not the forehead.

options.text: The hands only.
options.note: The blood is applied to ear, thumb, and toe together, not the hands alone.

options.text: No blood application is described.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly describes this application.

quiz.q: What provision does this chapter make for a healed person who is too poor to afford the standard offerings?

options.text: One male lamb as a guilt offering, and two turtledoves or two young pigeons for the sin and burnt offerings.
options.note: Verses 21 to 22 describe this scaled down provision for the poor.

options.text: He is exempt from any offering.
options.note: The law provides an accessible alternative rather than an exemption from all offerings.

options.text: He must wait until he can afford the full offering.
options.note: The provision allows immediate cleansing at a reduced cost, not a delay.

options.text: No provision is made for the poor.
options.note: Verses 21 to 32 give an extended, detailed provision for the poor.

quiz.q: What must happen to a house if mildew reappears after the contaminated stones have been removed and it has been replastered?

options.text: The house must be torn down completely, with its stones, timbers, and plaster taken outside the city.
options.note: Verses 43 to 45 describe this final measure for a house with destructive, persistent mildew.

options.text: The house is simply abandoned but left standing.
options.note: Verse 45 explicitly requires the house to be torn down and its materials removed, not merely abandoned.

options.text: The homeowner must live in it despite the danger.
options.note: The text requires the house's destruction, not continued habitation.

options.text: Nothing further is required.
options.note: Verses 43 to 45 give an explicit, further requirement.

quiz.q: What happens to the live bird at the end of both the person's and the house's cleansing ritual?

options.text: It is released into the open field or fields outside the city, carrying the blood of the slain bird.
options.note: Verses 7 and 53 both describe releasing the live bird after it has been dipped in the slain bird's blood.

options.text: It is also slaughtered, so that both birds die.
options.note: Only one bird is slaughtered; the other is deliberately kept alive and released, verses 7 and 53.

options.text: It is kept permanently by the priest.
options.note: The text explicitly describes the bird being released, not retained.

options.text: No live bird is used in this ritual.
options.note: Verses 4 to 7 and 49 to 53 explicitly describe the use of a live bird.

questions: This chapter shows God providing an elaborate, joyful ritual to welcome the healed person fully back into community and worship. Is there someone in your life who has been through a hard season and needs you to actively welcome them back, rather than just tolerate their return?

The healed person received the same ear, thumb, and toe ritual used to ordain priests, suggesting a restoration to full, whole hearted worship. What would it look like for you to offer your whole self, hearing, doing, and walking, back to God this week?

Even after physical healing, atonement still had to be made before the person was fully clean. Where in your own life have you experienced healing or improvement in circumstances, yet still need to deal honestly with a deeper spiritual issue underneath?

Hyssop, used here to apply cleansing blood, is the same plant David asked God to use to purge him from sin. When you feel the weight of guilt, do you actually ask God to cleanse you, or do you try to manage the guilt yourself?

God provided a real and generous path back for someone who had been excluded, showing that exclusion was never meant to be permanent. Where might God be calling you to extend that same kind of costly, generous welcome to someone others have written off?

translationNotes.note: The pairing of a slain bird and a live bird released afterward anticipates the two goats of the Day of Atonement in chapter 16, one sacrificed and one sent away, a recurring pattern of substitution and removal in Israel's ritual system.

The application of blood to the right earlobe, thumb, and big toe intentionally mirrors the ordination ritual of chapter 8:23-24, suggesting the healed person's full restoration to whole hearted worship and community life.

I put a contamination of mildew into a house attributes even this affliction to the LORD's sovereign hand, framing house mildew, like skin disease, as ultimately under God's governance rather than random misfortune, though the text does not elaborate a specific moral cause.
